content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662                322-5660              322-5901              322-6441              322-6444


April 27, 1982


Mr. Timothy J. Conlon
Manager, State Government
and Industry Affairs
Kaiser Aluminum & Chemical Corporation
300 Lakeside Drive
Oakland, CA 94643

RE: FPPC No. GC-82/133, A 82-073

Dear Mr. Conlon:

     Thank you for your letter and attachments dated March 24,
1982. We have reviewed the information you provided and
determined that Kaiser Aluminum & Chemical Corporation's
("Kaiser") expenditures in connection with the 1981 "Corporate
Issues" radio and television commercials are not "payments to
influence legislative or administrative action."

     During 1981, as part of a national advertising program,
Kaiser incurred $376,000 for production and broadcast of radio
and television commercials in California regarding "productivity"
and "energy conservation." The messages of the radio and
television commercials on each subject were identical. All
commercials concluded with a message urging people to write their
"elected representatives" and ask them to deal with these
issues. The television commercials were filmed with the U.S.
Capitol in the background. You have informed us that the primary
intent of these commercials was to encourage citizens to
participate in the democratic process on subjects of national,
instead of statewide, concern.

     Kaiser is a lobbyist employer and files disclosure reports
pursuant to Government Code Sections 86108 and 86109. As a
result, Kaiser is required to report, among other things,
"payments to influence legislative action during the period."
Section 82045 defines a "payment to influence legislative or
administrative action" to include:

Mr. Conlon
April 27, 1982
Page 2


     (e)  Payment for or in connection with
soliciting or urging other persons to enter into
direct communication with any elective state
official, legislative official or agency
official.

     The commercials were part of a nationwide advertising
program and did not specifically urge voters to contact any
California state official, legislative official, or agency
official regarding specific legislative or administrative
action. According to the information you provided, they were
designed to urge people, by inference, to contact their elected
federal representatives (congressmen and senators).  Under the
circumstances, we cannot conclude that they constitute a
solicitation to any persons to communicate with any California
state officials. Therefore, the cost of the commercials is not
a reportable payment pursuant to Sections 82045(e) and 86109.

     In order to eliminate any questions as to reporting
obligations in the future, however, we suggest that Kaiser
commercials identify which specific officials (federal, state,
or local) Kaiser is urging people to contact.

     If you have any further questions, please contact our Legal
Division at (916) 322-5901.

                                   Very truly yours,

                                   [signature]
                                   Alan Herndon
                                   Accounting Specialist
                                   Enforcement Division


AH:sf

KAISER ALUMINUM
CHEMICAL CORPORATION


March 24, 1982


Mr. Al Herndon, Investigator
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Al,

Thank you for your patience. Regarding your query about Kaiser
Aluminum's "Corporate Issues" radio and TV commercials, the enclosed
blue pamphlet describes the "Productivity" and "Energy" messages. The
Cost Summary sheet dated March 18 shows total production and broadcast
expenses for California were $376,000 in 1981. The radio and TV commer-
cials were aired in seven different "packages" from January 7 to
December. Because each commercial in a package is placed according to
a flexible telecast schedule, it is difficult to reconstruct when and
on what stations a precis radio or TV commercial was aired.

As I mentioned on the phone, these "issues" messages were con-
ceived to encourage the audience to participate in the democratic
process on subjects (work productivity and energy supply) of vital
concern to the entire nation. The concept and text were never intended
to encourage people to take a position on a specific legislative bill
or proposed regulation, either Federally or locally. As you can see
from our brochure, the commercials were prepared for national viewing,
with no California legislation or regulation in mind.

In our judgment these production and broadcast expenses shouldn't
be considered "employer of lobbyist" reportable expenses. We hope that
as you review this, you concur with this conclusion and that in the
recent spirit of the FPPC to streamline reports, you do not require
these kinds of expenses to be reported.

Thank you for your past assistance.

Sincerely,

Timothy J. Conlon, Manager
State Government & Industry Affairs

TJC:ph

March 18, 1982

KAISER ALUMINUM & CHEMICAL CORPORATION
1981 Corporate Issues Advertising
"Issues" Subjects
State of California

                             /a                         /b

  Energy Conservation        7             --           30           37
  Productivity               3             --           6            9
  Total, Radio               10            --           36           46

Television
  Energy Conservation        2             --           168          170
  Productivity               2             --           158          160
  Total, Television          4             --           326          330

Total, California "Issues"   14            --           362          376

1981 Total Corporate Issues
Program Expenditures         --            --           --           2,281

% "Issues" of Total
Program Expenditures         --            --           --           16.8%


/a     Production for television and radio commercials is purchased on a package basis.
       Figures shown represent an even distribution of costs for each commercial produced by
       job number (see attached).

/b     Broadcast buy-outs for television and radio are purchased on a package basis. Figures
       shown represent a distribution of costs estimated on an even rotation of commercials
       aired by job number (see attached).

Note: Commercials excluded from "Issues" —
       TV & Radio — Youth Employment, Difference is You,
       Economic Impact, special sports event.
       TV only — Aluminum Can, The Automobile


Y5101/yr

KAISER ALUMINUM
TAKES TO THE AIR.
Corporate Issues Program 1981

One Person Can Make
A Difference.

For 1981 our commitment to community involvement and issues of national concern grows even stronger. Our Corporate Issues Program will reach out to people in our major plant communities and Washington, D.C. in the most direct way possible. On television.

And for the first time, we will be seen not only on local network channels, but on satellite TV as well. Satellite TV is connected with cable networks wherein the picture is beamed not over the airwaves, but on a "signal" that is bounced to and from satellites lined up in the sky above the equator. For us that means the national audience of Ted Turner's pioneering Satellite Cable News Network will be seeing our messages.

What they and millions of other viewers will be seeing is a new appeal for them to speak out

on three crucial issues—productivity, energy
and youth employment. Three of our vice
presidents will be speaking to viewers in com-
mercials that were filmed on location in
Washington, D.C.

Two other television programs will be part of
our 1981 plan. Kaiser Aluminum will be one of
the major underwriters of a new Public Broad-
casting System series, "Hodding Carter's Inside
Story"—a show that features the distinguished
journalist and former Assistant Secretary of
State for Public Affairs critiquing the news cov-
erage of the media. In addition to our network
coverage, we will be sponsoring the Washington,
D.C. weekly showings of the "Nova" series.

Other routes we're taking this year include radio
and magazine ads. You'll see examples of these
messages on the following pages.

We strongly believe that our messages are more
timely than ever. There is a mood of change
n the country now. People need to be reminded
hat they can influence these changes. One
erson can make a difference

TELEVISION

TCOLINGER, V.P.
KAISER ALUMINUM

RON [illegible], V.P.
KAISER ALUMINUM

[illegible] V.P.
ALUMINUM

KAISER ALUMINUM
& CHEMICAL CORPORATION

KAISER ALUMINUM
& CHEMICAL CORPORATION

"PRODUCTIVITY"
Music Under
Tom Singer: The problem is declining productivity. It's caused by high energy costs, outmoded equipment and inadequate investments. But, by supporting those measures which encourage personal savings and investments, we could boost productivity again and reduce inflation and unemployment. At Kaiser Aluminum, we urge you to write your elected representatives to deal with this problem now. One person can make a difference. You.

Announcer: Kaiser Aluminum.

"ENERGY"
Music Under
Ron Rhody: It may be true that we're running low on oil. But it's absolutely false that we're running low on energy. At Kaiser Aluminum we believe America has plenty of energy resources—coal, nuclear, solar, hydro and others. But we must remove the roadblocks to their development. Will we? It's up to you. Write your elected representatives and urge them to deal with these problems now.

One person can make a difference. You.

Announcer: Kaiser Aluminum.

"YOUTH EMPLOYMENT"
Music Under
Bob Turner: It's time we stopped crying Uncle. We can't keep turning only to Uncle Sam to help us out. For instance, youth unemployment now stands at about 18%. For minority youth it's almost 38%. At Kaiser Aluminum we believe that isn't just government's problem. It's everyone's. If you're in a position to hire, give our young people a chance.

One person can make a difference. You.

Announcer: For more ideas on youth jobs call for our booklet. Kaiser Aluminum.

RADIO

The music "The Difference Is You" is again our theme for a radio campaign localized for the Bay Area. Between verses of the messages on a announcer will deliver our messages on productivity, energy and youth employment.

PRINT

IT'S TIME BUSINESS
STOPPED MINDING ITS
OWN BUSINESS.

The business of business is more than just profits. Every executive today is well of this. Board are being barraged with the pressing problems of unemployment, crime, housing and the economics, education, health and the country's expansion. These are matters of serious concern. But we are most people in the business community are concerned as they are. What's really being solved these problems as though they were just supply portion employment programs. They're actually involving themselves.

Energy conscious business people are broadening their company's conservation programs out into local communities. They're actively participating in their area's battle against crime. They're preparing new directions in this same as well as the improvement of educational standards. The improvement of education is our responsibility. We are alive to a realism in our community and economics. We as able to make leadership, in exactly what the training of people in business it is our abilities to solve today's problems people so this country is the exactly what the training of people in business today to help solve this country's problems.

[Kaiser Aluminum & Chemical Corporation logo]
I PERSON
CAN MAKE A
DIFFERENCE

BUSINESS

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Kaiser Aluminum & Chemical Corporation's $376,000 expenditure on 1981 'Corporate Issues' radio and television commercials urging citizens to contact elected representatives on productivity and energy conservation constitutes reportable 'payments to influence legislative or administrative action' under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Kaiser Aluminum's commercial expenditures are not reportable payments under Political Reform Act Sections 82045(e) and 86109 because the commercials, as part of a nationwide program, did not specifically urge voters to contact California state, legislative, or agency officials regarding specific legislative or administrative action, but rather urged contact with federal representatives on national issues.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains incoming request letter from Kaiser (March 24, 1982) followed by FPPC response letter (April 27, 1982). Analyzed as advice letter based on FPPC's formal conclusion. Cost summary and marketing materials included as supporting documents. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers present but content clearly structured as Q&A format.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Kaiser Aluminum & Chemical Corporation's $376,000 expenditure on 1981 'Corporate Issues' radio and television commercials urging citizens to contact elected representatives on productivity and energy conservation constitutes reportable 'payments to influence legislative or administrative action' under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: Kaiser Aluminum's commercial expenditures are not reportable payments under Political Reform Act Sections 82045(e) and 86109 because the commercials, as part of a nationwide program, did not specifically urge voters to contact California state, legislative, or agency officials regarding specific legislative or administrative action, but rather urged contact with federal representatives on national issues.

The messages of the radio and television commercials on each subject were identical. All commercials concluded with a message urging people to write their "elected representatives" and ask them to deal with these issues. The television commercials were filmed with the U.S. Capitol in the background. You have informed us that the primary intent of these commercials was to encourage citizens to participate in the democratic process on subjects of national, instead of statewide, concern. The commercials were part of a nationwide advertising program and did not specifically urge voters to contact any California state official, legislative official, or agency official regarding specific legislative or administrative action. embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter determining that Kaiser Aluminum's $376,000 corporate issues advertising campaign featuring national concerns (productivity and energy conservation) does not constitute reportable lobbyist employer payments because the commercials did not specifically target California state or local officials, though the FPPC recommends future commercials identify specific officials to eliminate reporting ambiguity.
